title: Property, Plant and Equipment
description: Premium Analysis Guide: AS vs Ind AS vs IFRS vs US GAAP

sections.sectionTitle: SECTION A: Core Technical Comparison Table
tableData: Focuses on historical cost with limited revaluation use.
Cost model or revaluation model, with component accounting.
Same broad model as Ind AS.
Mostly historical cost, with some limited fair value options.
From simple cost tracking to more judgment-based asset measurement.
PPE recognized when future economic benefits are probable and cost can be measured reliably.
At cost, including purchase price, non-refundable taxes, freight, installation, and directly attributable costs.
Cost includes all directly attributable costs.
Usually carried at cost less depreciation and impairment.
Cost less depreciation and impairment; revaluation model generally not allowed for PPE.
Ind AS/IFRS allow fair value uplift through revaluation.
Based on useful life and residual value; component accounting less emphasized historically.
Component accounting is also required where appropriate.
More precise matching of cost to consumption.
Estimates are updated more actively under Ind AS/IFRS.
Reviewed periodically, usually when indicators change.
More frequent reassessment under Ind AS/IFRS.
ASC 360: recoverability test, then impairment loss if carrying value exceeds fair value.
Different impairment mechanics can affect timing and size of losses.
Generally capitalized when directly attributable to qualifying assets.
Capitalize directly attributable borrowing costs during construction.
Construction assets often carry interest into cost.
Often recognized based on specific guidance, sometimes netted against asset cost.
IAS 20-style treatment: deferred income or deduction from asset cost depending on policy.
Grant accounting affects opening PPE carrying value.
Certain FX differences on foreign currency borrowings may be capitalized in some cases.
FX treatment follows US GAAP foreign currency rules.
Capitalization of FX is more limited and rule-based.
Allowed for classes of PPE; revaluation surplus goes to OCI.
Major divergence between Ind AS/IFRS and US GAAP.
Asset recognized at cost + freight. ARO usually ignored or cash basis. Depreciation generally WDV or SLM over schedule 2 rates.
Cost + Freight + PV of ARO capitalized. Component accounting mandatory. Depreciation matches consumption profile precisely.
Cost + ARO capitalized. Component accounting applied. Impairment models differ slightly (recoverability test).

sections.sectionTitle: SECTION D: Side-by-Side Impact Table (Project Only)
SECTION F: Balance Sheet Impact - End of Year 1
SECTION G: Cash Flow Impact - Year 1
SECTION I: Earnings Quality - Signals Table
tableData: More complete capitalization of PPE and obligations.
ROA and turnover may look weaker due to larger denominator.
Compare returns on a PPE-adjusted basis.
Cost is spread more systematically over useful life.
Depreciation is now more aligned with actual consumption.
Check whether useful lives and residuals are realistic.
Carrying value exceeds recoverable amount or fair value.
Management may have overestimated asset life or future usage.
Fair value uplift is recognized in OCI rather than P&L.
Balance sheet may look stronger without immediate cash impact.
Separate accounting uplift from operating performance.
Future outflow is now recognized today.
Hidden end-of-life costs are becoming visible.
Check whether the liability is fully measured.
Use comparable asset base across peers.
Separate revaluation reserve from earned equity.
Lower turnover may reflect accounting, not weaker demand.
Include restoration and other obs in leverage ratios.
Leverage is more truthful, not worse quality.
Equity improved on paper, not in cash.
Largely timing: same PPE components, recognized over time now. Growth moved from X% to Y%.
Same total project profit; cash flows unchanged.
Lifetime margins unchanged; only period allocation changed. Margin moved from X% to Y%.
"Are PPE component assets riskier than inventory?"
No - represent enforceable rights to payment.
"Are IFRS and US GAAP outcomes different?"
For these PPE components, Ind AS, IFRS and ASC 360 are identical.
Rebuild normalized earnings and re-test value.
Read transition note, focus on reported EPS.
Increase weight if they trust management.
Ask for pre/post-transition bridges, model normalized.
Adjust for timing; ensure covenant headroom valid.
Emphasising "strong rev growth" without explaining standard
🟡Yellow flag - demand bridge to old basis.
Likely higher earnings quality; fewer hidden issues.
Avoiding disclosure of PPE component assets breakdown
May be hiding loss-making or delayed projects.
Explicitly stating "IFRS, Ind AS and US GAAP are the same"

sections.sectionTitle: SECTION P: Red Flags vs Green Flags (Visual Indicator Matrix)
tableData: Frequent changes to estimated useful lives to boost immediate profit
Quality of earnings is very low. Normalize depreciation to historical averages.
Capitalizing major repairs that were previously expensed
Artificial margin padding. Strip capitalized repairs from EBITDA.
Gross block balloons while revenue stagnates (falling asset turnover)
Capital deployed is generating no returns. Flag for severe future impairment risk.
Clean separation and componentization of major assets
Trust the reported maintenance capex figures as realistic.
Depreciation policy precisely matches the economic life of assets
P&L reflects true economic burn rate of capital.
